title: Set Cover (Service Teams)
summary: Cover every location using the fewest teams, given each team covers a known subset.
problem_statement: I need to cover four locations {a, b, c, d} using as few service teams as possible. Team S1 covers {a, b}, Team S2 covers {b, c}, Team S3 covers {c, d}, Team S4 covers {a, d}. Which teams should I send?
real_world_example: Choosing which polling stations to staff so every neighborhood is reachable, or selecting backup data centers so every region has at least one within range.

expected_pattern.variables: x_i in {0, 1} — whether team i is selected
expected_pattern.constraints: for each location, sum of selected teams covering it >= 1

expected_solution_summary: Two teams suffice: {S1, S3} (covers a,b + c,d). The complementary partition {S2, S4} also gives cost 2.
learning_notes: Set cover is the textbook 'multi-constraint covering' QUBO. Each location turns into one >= constraint. The penalty form is more delicate than knapsack's single capacity: too low a Lagrange and the solver leaves a location uncovered; too high and the energy landscape becomes a wall of penalty terms.

learning_objectives: Encode 'every X must be touched by at least one Y' as a family of >= 1 inequalities
Develop intuition for Lagrange-multiplier tuning when you have many small constraints
Recognize covering patterns hiding under different domain names (scheduling, facility location)
